hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: No fanfare. No India launch event. Just the first frontier model that doesn't lose the plot when you switch languages mid-sentence. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: Claude Opus 4.8 quietly clears a reasoning benchmark by 60% — and finally handles Hinglish without dropping context.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Why your fine-tuned model gets worse after every retraining — and the cheaper fix nobody runs.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real number on AI-eval roles in India, plus the senior-IC question doing the rounds this month.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: What an English-trained frontier model still drops the moment you write the way India actually talks.

one_thing.head: Claude 4.8 is the first frontier model that doesn't break on Hinglish
one_thing.lede_html:
An engineer in Bengaluru types this into Claude 4.8, mid-debug, half-frustrated: yaar yeh function crash kar raha hai jab user ka input contains special characters, kya tu suggest karega — Hindi verb, English noun, no transliteration, no preamble. She hits enter expecting the usual ritual: model picks the wrong language, hallucinates a Devanagari helper file, or politely asks her to retype in English. Instead, the reply lands clean. The code suggestion is in Python. The explanation is in the same Hinglish she wrote in.

Think of every frontier model before this as a violinist who can play in two keys but freezes for half a beat each time the score modulates. The pause is small. You barely notice it on simple phrases. But on a real Indian sentence — three modulations in six words — the freeze stacks into a full break. GPT-4o split the languages. Gemini Pro transliterated when it shouldn't have. 4.8 just kept playing.

Everyone clipped the headline number: 60% jump on a new reasoning benchmark. Real, worth a tweet. The quieter result — the one Indian devs surfaced by Wednesday on their own production prompts — is that for the first time, the modulation doesn't break the music. It's not Indic-specialised. It just stopped failing the way every other frontier model fails. Quiet, huge, almost nobody covered it.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: hot takes calling 4.8 "a new era". Why: it's a real upgrade, not a phase shift — treat it like a tool, not a moment.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: If your product takes Indian-user input, run your top 20 production prompts through 4.8 this weekend. The wins will be where you've been hacking — translation pre-passes, language detection, transliteration. Most of that scaffolding is now net cost, not net help.
lenses.action: → Do: A/B 4.8 vs your current model on 50 real Hinglish queries. Cut the pre-translation step where 4.8 wins on its own.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: The Indic-language gap was a strategic lever for some Indian products — "we work for the next-billion users, they don't". 4.8 narrows that gap, doesn't close it. Update your competitive frame: Indic-fluency is now a tier, not a moat. The defensible play moves one layer up — proprietary Indian data, distribution, trust.
lenses.action: → This quarter: rewrite your one-pager. Replace "Indic-language advantage" with the specific data, partner, or workflow you own that 4.8 can't touch.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: Your team will read the 4.8 numbers and want to rip up the roadmap. Don't — not yet. If you're mid-career, the habit worth starting is a "tried, kept, deferred" note per quarter — one line for what 4.8 lets you ship now, one for what stays as-is, one for what you'll reassess next quarter. If you're senior, the harder move is the "not this quarter" list — three things 4.8 makes tempting but not yet wise. Mine for your team: (1) Ripping out the translation/transliteration layer — keep it for one more quarter; 4.8 still degrades on long mixed-script prompts. (2) Re-running the Indic-language eval suite from scratch — your existing one is fine, add 4.8 as a column. (3) Switching default model for production traffic — pilot on internal tools first, watch latency and cost per call before you flip customers. That one-pager is your contribution this week. It's worth more than any prototype.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write the "tried, kept, deferred" one-pager — one line each — by Friday. Senior: write the 3-item "not this quarter" list and the one sentence you'd defend it with in the CTO review. Send by Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: If you speak any Indian language, you have a real, undervalued skill right now: spotting where frontier models still break for Indian users. You don't need to code to do this — you need to test.
lenses.action: → First rung: run 10 Hinglish prompts (the way you'd actually type to a friend) through Claude 4.8 and one other model. Note where each breaks. That's a portfolio artifact — "India-eval auditor" — built in 45 min.

build_notes.title: Your fine-tuned model gets worse after every retraining. Here's why — and the cheaper fix.
build_notes.paper_ref: Kotha et al., "Understanding Catastrophic Forgetting in Sequential Fine-Tuning" (NeurIPS 2025) + the LoRA-merging follow-ups
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: every time you fine-tune on this month's data, the model quietly forgets last month's. It's called catastrophic forgetting — and full fine-tuning makes it worse than LoRA-style adapters. Fix: stop full-retraining monthly. Run a small LoRA per data slice and merge at inference. Ship metric: evaluate every new fine-tune on the oldest eval set you have, not just the freshest.
build_notes.struggle_html: A CX lead drops a Loom in the channel on a Thursday. A B2B customer who's been on your support bot since launch just got told her contract-renewal query is "outside my scope" — a query type the model handled fluently in March. Six monthly fine-tunes later, the bot has forgotten she exists. This month's eval scored 91%. Last month's was 87%. Every number you measure says the model is improving. Every ticket in her thread says it isn't.
build_notes.finding_html: Think of the model as a library with one fixed-size shelf. Every month an archivist clears space for new acquisitions by pulling the oldest, least-requested books — quietly, no log, no ceremony. Month six, a reader asks for a launch-era title and the archivist returns empty-handed. Nothing was deleted on purpose. The shelf just kept getting rewritten. That's full fine-tuning: every cycle adjusts the same weights, so each new pass overwrites a slice of the last one. Bigger learning rate, sharper overwrites. Kotha et al. (NeurIPS 2025) named it catastrophic forgetting and measured the curve. The model isn't broken. The shelf was never extended.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves you make on Monday. First, stop letting the archivist near the original shelf. Freeze the base model and bolt on a LoRA adapter per data slice — one annex for support tickets, one for sales, one for compliance. Each annex is its own room; nothing on the launch shelf moves. Second, do the assembly at inference, not at training: merge the relevant adapters only when a query arrives, so any single annex can be weighted down, swapped, or rolled back without disturbing the rest. The launch-era titles stay exactly where the reader left them. Sales drift? Patch the sales annex. The 91% B2B-contract behaviour from launch survives untouched.
build_notes.metric_html: Kotha et al. measured it: on a 4-slice sequential fine-tune, accuracy on the first slice dropped from 84% → 51% with full FT, but held at 82% with merged LoRAs. Same compute budget.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Pick your oldest eval set — the one from your first fine-tune. Run every new model on it before you ship. If the number drops more than 3 points, you're forgetting, not improving. That's your retention metric.

code.body:
from peft import PeftModel

# one adapter per data slice, all merged at inference
base = load_base("claude-opus-4-8")
m = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "adapters/support")
m.load_adapter("adapters/sales",      adapter_name="sales")
m.load_adapter("adapters/compliance", adapter_name="compliance")

m.add_weighted_adapter(
    adapters=["support", "sales", "compliance"],
    weights=[0.4, 0.4, 0.2],   # tune per route
    adapter_name="merged",
    combination_type="linear",
)
m.set_adapter("merged")        # base weights untouched → old slices preserved

rows.what_html: Senior IC roles (Staff / Principal AI) are pulling away from manager tracks in Indian comp bands — the curve has steepened sharply this quarter. The market is paying for depth, not headcount.

"Indic-language evals" is the new line item in JDs — basically, you build test sets that prove a model works for Hindi/Tamil/Hinglish users, not just English. Almost nobody has done this in production. If you have, name it.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-evaluation roles across Indian product companies and GCCs this quarter — up ~3× from Q1
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM AI Talent Tracker, June 2026
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "AI evaluation" OR "model evaluation" OR "LLM evals" + (Bengaluru OR Hyderabad OR Pune) and read 5 JDs end-to-end. Start with the careers pages of Razorpay, Sarvam AI, and Flipkart — all three are hiring evaluation engineers right now, and the JDs read like a syllabus.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Pick one AI assistant you already use. Write 15 prompts in pure Hinglish — the way you actually text a friend. Mix Devanagari and roman script. Include 5 prompts that need code-switching mid-sentence. Run them through Claude 4.8 and one other model. Note every place each one breaks.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Read AI4Bharat's IndicBench overview (free, on their site) + Anthropic's "evaluating model outputs" cookbook. 75 minutes, total. You'll know more about Indic-language evaluation than 95% of resumes claiming "AI experience".

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 15 prompts, side-by-side outputs, and one line per failure on what category of error it is (translation, transliteration, context loss, code-switch). That doc is your portfolio. When they ask "have you worked on Indic AI?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Next week's rung builds on this one. We climb together, one issue at a time. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q: "Your fine-tuned support model has been retrained monthly for six months. This month's eval: 91%, up from 87% last month. Customer escalations: up 35%, all on query types your team doesn't think about anymore (refund policy nuances, B2B contract terms, ticket-categorization rules from launch). Engineering wants to keep iterating. CX wants you to revert. You have one week to call it. Walk me through how you'd diagnose what's actually happening, decide, and what you'd ship as the fix."

steps.body_html: Audit the eval drift. Pull every eval set used across the six retraining cycles and tag each query by class — refunds, B2B contracts, categorisation, current month's themes. The eval doesn't include classes the team has stopped thinking about, which is exactly where production still lives. Naming the missing classes is the diagnosis.

Measure oldest-set retention. Take the eval set from month one — the launch-era queries — and run every successive fine-tune on it. If accuracy on that set has dropped more than three points across cycles, you're forgetting, not improving. This is the retention metric Build Notes proposes; it surfaces catastrophic forgetting that average accuracy hides by construction.

Diagnose the forgetting class. Catastrophic forgetting (Kotha et al., NeurIPS 2025) means full fine-tuning rewrites the same weights every cycle, so each pass overwrites the previous one. Six cycles of monthly full FT have layered six overwrites — the launch-era classes are the most degraded because they've been overwritten the most times. The complaint pattern matches that decay curve exactly.

Isolate with adapters, then set a retention SLO. Ship a LoRA adapter for the most-forgotten class against a frozen base, merge at inference — old behaviour preserved by construction. Then commit, in writing, to a retention SLO: no model promotes if the oldest eval set drops more than three points. That converts a one-time fix into a forgetting-class guardrail.

interview.tip_html: The downlevel version of this answer treats inbox and dashboard as a tie you break — pick the user reports, partial-revert, ship a hotfix, move on. That's the answer for a tier-2 outage. This isn't one. An eval rising while complaints rise means the rubric has aged out of the conversation in the same direction the model has, and that has to be named in your first thirty seconds. Roll v9 back to v8 on the worst-degraded class against a frozen base while you build a LoRA adapter. Pull last quarter's eval set off cold storage and rerun v9 against it; the gap is your retention number. Write the retention SLO — threshold, owner, override path — and put it in front of the head of training before the next cycle. The hotfix buys this week. The SLO is what means the question never reaches the board.
interview.framework_name: Audit the eval drift → Measure oldest-set retention → Diagnose the forgetting class → Isolate with adapters → Set a retention SLO
interview.why_they_ask_html: This is a measurement-reframe test wearing a rollout-decision costume. The two numbers handed to you point in opposite directions on purpose — eval up, escalations up — and the candidate who reads them as a user-behaviour problem ("customers are wrong") or a regression problem ("the new fine-tune is bad") has missed the diagnosis: the eval suite and the model have forgotten the same classes, in the same order, because both reflect what the team currently thinks about. An Anthropic, OpenAI, or DeepMind interviewer is watching for three moves in one breath — reframe the conflict as eval drift, decide under the one-week clock without taking engineering's or CX's side, and ship something that prevents the class of failure rather than this month's instance. How you sound under deadline is what gets logged.
interview.sample_answer_html:
Let me start with the conflict in the numbers, because the room is reading it wrong. Eval up, escalations up, on classes the team has stopped thinking about — that's not engineering versus CX. That's the eval and the model forgetting the same classes, together, for the same reason. [why this works: the reframe is the entire interview. Landing it in thirty seconds tells the interviewer you treat measurement as a hypothesis, not ground truth.]

So my week-one move isn't a decision, it's a diagnostic. Pull every eval set from the six monthly retrainings and tag queries by class — refund nuances, B2B contract terms, launch-era categorisation. My bet is the eval has migrated toward whatever the team is focused on now, and the launch-era classes are barely represented. If they're not in the eval, no fine-tune is being penalised for losing them.

Step two — the metric that should have existed for the last four cycles — assemble an oldest eval set. Sample around 200 queries from month-one production, balanced across the classes CX is escalating on, and run every successive fine-tune on it. If accuracy on that set has dropped more than three points across cycles, the diagnosis is locked: catastrophic forgetting, not user error. [why this works: naming the threshold (three points) and sample size (200) before the result signals you've instrumented a retention check, not just read about one.]

I'd expect the curve Kotha et al. described — full fine-tuning rewrites the same weights every cycle, so six cycles in, the launch-era classes have been overwritten six times. The complaint pattern matches the decay curve. [why this works: pattern-matching the production symptom to a named failure mode under a real citation reads as "PM who can name what's wrong on a whiteboard".]

The fix is structural. Ship a LoRA adapter for the most-forgotten class — refund nuances first, by escalation volume — against a frozen base, merge at inference. Frozen base means launch-era behaviour is preserved by construction. Adapter per slice, swappable, no full retrain to recover what's lost.

The call for this week: partial revert to last month's model for the affected classes while the adapter is prepared, keep this month's model for everything else, and write a retention SLO before either ships. No model promotes if the oldest set drops more than three points, owner named, four-week horizon for re-anchoring. [why this works: volunteering the SLO unasked is what an interviewer logs as "ships in classes, not in incidents".] Engineering keeps iterating on adapters. CX gets the regression handled. Neither side wins because the argument was never the right one.
interview.eval_deep_dive_html:
Most candidates know to log production traffic. Almost none know what to evaluate against it — and that gap is the whole reason catastrophic forgetting goes unflagged for six cycles in a row.

The oldest-eval-set is a fixed slice of around 200 queries sampled from the LAUNCH window — weeks one, two, three of real customer traffic, before the team built any opinions about what mattered. Stratify it across every product surface the launch version covered: categorisation, refund-rule lookups, B2B-contract terms. Then archive it. Every fine-tune ships with this exact set scored — never patched to flatter the current model, never curated when a class gets retired from the roadmap. The instant you let the eval drift along with the team's attention, you've reproduced the catastrophic-forgetting failure mode in the eval surface itself, which is precisely what blinded everyone in the question.

Score it as retention, not improvement. retention = min(cohort_accuracy[c] / launch_baseline[c] for c in cohorts) — the per-cohort hit rate divided by the launch-era baseline, aggregated as min-over-cohorts. Average hides a single class collapsing; min-over-cohorts is what surfaces it. The threshold lives in version control, not a Slack thread: a >3-point drop on any cohort blocks promotion, citing Kotha et al. NeurIPS 2025 from Build Notes. Pre-commit precedes rollout, always.

Run two evals in parallel — the immutable retention set (same questions forever, monthly trend) and a rolling current-eval that refreshes monthly to track product surface. A model that wins on current and regresses on retention is the diagnostic signature of forgetting. If a launch-era cohort shrinks below ~30 queries because the surface was retired, retire the cohort from the metric, not the set.

Plot the output on two axes: retention (min-cohort) on Y, current accuracy on X. Healthy fine-tunes move up-and-right; forgetting fine-tunes move up-and-left. Any retention drop >3pts holds the rollout; >5pts triggers a roll-back of the previous fine-tune. PM owns the SLO. ML lead can override with written justification and a one-week sunset. CX gets read access — same data, no veto.
interview.depth_guide_html: Open with the reframe — eval up plus complaints up equals eval drift, not user error — and the framework name in one breath. That's the load-bearing move; everything else is mechanism. Hold for the probe: the oldest-set sample size and three-point threshold maths, the LoRA architecture (frozen base, adapter per slice, inference-time merge), and the SLO governance question on who signs and who can override. Lead with mechanics, you sound like an engineer; lead with the reframe, you sound like a PM.

counters.q: Engineering's read is that this month's eval improvement is real and CX is just noisy. How do you get them to accept the eval itself is the problem?
counters.strong_html: I wouldn't argue the eval — I'd extend it. Take engineering's current eval as given, add the oldest-eval-set as a parallel scoreboard, and run this month's model on both. If accuracy holds on the new set and drops more than three points on the old one, the conversation is over without anyone having to say the current eval is wrong. Engineering loses no credit; the suite just gains a retention dimension. Faster than persuasion, and the data shows up where the meeting actually happens.
counters.weak_html: Walk engineering through the catastrophic-forgetting paper and explain why monthly full fine-tunes have this failure mode by construction.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Citing a paper into a measurement fight is how the meeting becomes a graduate seminar. Engineering doesn't accept a theory result over their own eval numbers, and they shouldn't — they should accept a second eval set running side-by-side with the first one. The strong move adds evidence; the weak move asks for belief.

counters.q: You have one week and two senior stakeholders pulling opposite ways. How do you call it without picking a side?
counters.strong_html: Reframe the decision so neither side has to lose. Partial revert for the affected classes is the CX request, scoped down to the actual regressed classes, not a full rollback that throws away this month's gains. LoRA adapter on the frozen base for the worst-forgotten class is the engineering move, just structurally different from another full fine-tune. The retention SLO is the governance move that says we don't have this fight again. Put all three on one page, name who owns each, ask the room to disagree with the structure, not the call.
counters.weak_html: Run a tiebreaker meeting with the VP and present both sides, then implement whichever the VP picks.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Escalating to the VP is the move that announces you can't hold conflicting senior input on your own. Interviewers at hire-bar AI orgs are screening for whether the PM is the decision-shaper or the message-carrier; punting to the VP places you firmly in the second bucket, regardless of which call comes back.

counters.q: Why not just run another full fine-tune that re-includes the forgotten classes? It's the cheapest move and engineering is already set up for it.
counters.strong_html: Because it doesn't solve the class of failure, it resets the timer on it. Full fine-tuning rewrites the same weights every cycle; adding the forgotten classes back this month means a different class quietly falls off next month when the next data slice gets ingested. Kotha showed a 4-slice sequential full FT degraded slice-one accuracy 84% to 51% while merged LoRAs held 82% on the same compute. The architectural move costs roughly the same engineering time as another full retrain and removes the failure mode permanently.
counters.weak_html: We'd run a full fine-tune with the forgotten classes weighted higher in the training mix.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Re-weighting treats catastrophic forgetting as a sampling bug instead of an architectural one. It works for one cycle, then the next slice introduces a new under-weighted class and the curve restarts. Senior candidates at AI labs treat the failure mode as the thing to design out, not as a quarterly calibration.

counters.q: Who owns the retention SLO, who can override it, and how do you set the threshold? Give me the governance.
counters.strong_html: Owner is the AI PM — me — because the SLO is the operational definition of quality, and that's a product call. Override is a written exception co-signed by engineering lead, CX lead, and AI PM; no single function can promote a model that breaches the threshold. Threshold is set jointly with CX off the historical complaint-to-accuracy-drop curve — three points on the oldest set, because that's the gap correlating with the escalation lift now. Re-anchored each quarter as the oldest set rotates, previous thresholds logged so we can see if we're loosening the bar over time.
counters.weak_html: Engineering owns it as a CI check, and any number we breach automatically blocks the deploy.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: A CI block with no sign-off path gets bypassed within six weeks by a flag-flip the first time the team wants to ship under pressure. Real SLO governance has an override path on paper so the exceptions are visible; the weak version has it in someone's head, which is the same as not having one.

traps.move: Trusting this month's eval improvement at face value
traps.signal_lost_html: You've skipped the entire diagnosis. Once you accept the 91% as evidence the model is improving, you've conceded that the conflict between eval and escalations is a CX problem to be managed instead of an eval-drift problem to be measured. The interviewer logs "does not interrogate the metric" and the rest of the answer is execution on the wrong premise.

traps.move: Recommending a full revert based on CX volume alone
traps.signal_lost_html: Full revert throws away six months of legitimate gains on classes that are currently working, in order to fix a regression on a smaller cohort. It's the move that reads as taking CX's side under pressure rather than holding both numbers in tension. Senior PMs revert by class, not by model — and they say so unprompted.

traps.move: Proposing "we'll fix it in the next training cycle"
traps.signal_lost_html: This is the answer that treats catastrophic forgetting as a one-time bug instead of a property of the training pipeline. The next cycle will introduce a different forgetting class because the architecture hasn't changed. Interviewers register this as the candidate who has never operated a sequential fine-tune in production — they've only shipped one and moved on.

traps.move: Asking for two more weeks to do the diagnosis properly
traps.signal_lost_html: The one-week constraint is part of the question. Asking for more time signals you can't compress a diagnosis under a real deadline — the most-tested PM dimension at any AI lab. The right move is to scope the diagnosis to fit the week: oldest-set sample of 200, one adapter for the worst class, SLO on a four-week horizon for full re-anchoring.

interview.good_vs_great_html: The interviewer asks, two minutes in: "Say more about the SLO — what's the threshold?" The downlevel candidate answers the question asked: a number, a justification, a line about monitoring. The hire-bar candidate answers the question behind it. "Before the number — the SLO owner sits on the training team, not on PM, and override requires the head of training and the head of risk in the same room. The number is a 5% retention floor on the last four quarters' eval sets, breached for two consecutive cycles. I'd defend the floor in design review, not in incident review." Who can move the threshold, and when, is what the interviewer was probing for.
interview.meta_skill_html: What separates a PM who ships the hotfix from one who ships the rule the hotfix is an instance of? Hearing two metrics moving in the same direction and asking what the measurement system has stopped seeing, instead of which metric to trust.
interview.teaser_q: Eval up to 91% this month. Customer escalations up 35%, all on query types nobody's measuring anymore. Engineering wants to iterate; CX wants to revert. You have one week.
interview.q_html:
Your fine-tuned support model has been retrained monthly for six months.

This month's eval: 91%, up from 87% last month. Customer escalations: up 35%, all on query types your team doesn't think about anymore — refund-policy nuances, B2B contract terms, ticket-categorisation rules from launch.

Engineering wants to keep iterating. CX wants you to revert. You have one week to call it.

Walk me through how you'd diagnose what's actually happening, decide, and what you'd ship as the fix.

under_the_hood.question_html: You paste a 30-page document into Claude, ask a question about line 4 of page 2, and the model answers using something from page 28 instead. Frustrating. Familiar. Everyone keeps calling this a "context window" problem — as if the fix is buying a bigger one. But you just gave it the line. It saw the line. It just didn't notice the line. So what is a context window actually?
under_the_hood.diagram_svg: <svg class="hood-svg" viewBox="0 0 600 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Diagram: a context window is like one whiteboard the model can see all at once. It can read every word, but only weighs a few of them strongly — and once erased between sessions, it remembers nothing."><defs><style>.lbl{font-family:'Archivo text-anchor="middle" class="sub">Bigger whiteboard = more it can <tspan font-style="italic">see</tspan>. It still chooses what to <tspan font-style="italic">notice</tspan>. Wipe the board between chats — gone.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: Stand the model in front of a single huge whiteboard. Your prompt, the chat history, the 30 pages you pasted — every word sits on that board at once. When marketing says "200k context", they mean a bigger board, not sharper eyes. A second mechanism, called attention, then walks the board and decides which patches of ink to weigh heavily. The board is the room. Attention is the reader inside the room. Size buys floor space; it doesn't choose what gets read.

steps.title: Why bigger isn't always better
steps.body_html: Stretch the board to ten metres wide. The reader still has the same two eyes. Researchers have a name for what happens next — "lost in the middle." Patches near the top-left corner and the bottom-right get noticed; the patches in the dead centre quietly fade into background. Your line 4 of page 2 was right there in the middle, and the reader's eyes skipped past it to a sharper patch near the end. More room dilutes the signal you cared about — it doesn't amplify it.

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: Stop fighting attention; arrange the board for it. When the AI misses something obvious, the move is rarely "paste more." Put the line that matters at the top or the bottom of your prompt, restate it once in your own words, and prune the noise in between. You can't change the reader's eyes. You can absolutely change where you write on the board. Position is a lever you control. Window size isn't.

source.text: Decoded from: Liu et al., "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" (TACL 2024)

the_rep.lite_html: Build the smallest useful thing this weekend: paste your last 5 customer-support emails into Claude 4.8 and ask it to draft a one-line reply for each, in the same tone as your previous replies. Read the drafts. Keep the two that need almost no editing.
the_rep.full_html: Take it one step further: write a 12-line script (or use Claude's project feature) that does this on a schedule — every morning, it reads yesterday's incoming emails and drafts replies into a doc you skim with coffee. You're not building a product. You're building one personal workflow that saves you 30 minutes a day. That's the whole Rep.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: a working draft pipeline you used for one full day, and an honest count of how many drafts were good enough to send with one edit. Reply with the count — featured results are shared with permission.

reader_win.quote: Built the draft pipeline on Saturday. Monday I went through 27 emails in 18 minutes. 14 drafts I sent with one edit. Felt like cheating, in a good way.

toolbox.tool_html: The "two-language gut-check" trick. When you write something important in English — a proposal, a customer message, a policy line — paste it into your AI and ask: "Translate this into simple Hindi the way someone would actually speak it, then translate that Hindi back into English." The round-trip strips out jargon you didn't notice. The version that survives is the version your reader actually understands.
toolbox.try_html: If the Hindi version sounds awkward, your English original is too clever. Plain-language audit, free.

reality_check.h3: The benchmark says "60% better". For Indian-language users, the picture is messier.
reality_check.body_html:
Lakshmi is a homemaker in Chennai. She opens her bank's new AI assistant on a Wednesday morning and types: enga kitta home loan eligibility check pannunga, monthly income 65000, EMI already 18000. Tamil syntax, English nouns — the way half of urban Tamil Nadu actually messages. The model answers in clean English, runs the math correctly, and quotes her an interest rate based on a state she doesn't live in. She retypes the city name in Tamil script. This time it tells her the figures aren't supported in her region and offers an 8-12 minute wait for a human. She closes the app.

IndicBench-2's 2025 evaluation across 11 Indian languages found even top-tier models lost 22–38% of their English accuracy on the same reasoning task translated into Hindi, Tamil, or Bengali — and the drop got sharper for code-switched input, the way most Indian users actually type. Claude 4.8 narrows that gap on Hinglish. It does not close it for Tamil-English, Bangla-English, or any of the other 20-odd switching pairs Indian users live in. The assistant didn't fail loudly for Lakshmi. It failed by quietly handing her back to a queue.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: Claude 4.8's Hinglish improvement is real — and still partial. It's not a reason to distrust the model; it's a reason to evaluate it for your users, not someone else's benchmark. The fix isn't doom, it's discipline: build a 100-prompt eval in the languages your users actually speak, and re-run it every time a model upgrades. The bias doesn't go away. It just gets harder to see.
reality_check.source: Source: AI4Bharat & IIIT-Hyderabad, "IndicBench-2: Cross-Lingual Reasoning Evaluation" (2025); language-coverage figures cross-checked against Anthropic's June 2026 model card.

cards.h4: BYJU'S successor Eruditus quietly running AI tutors in 700 Maharashtra schools
cards.body: No flashy launch. A regional pilot with the state board — AI tutors that switch between English and Marathi mid-explanation, based on the student's last reply. One of the largest Marathi-language AI deployments anywhere.
cards.why_you: this is what "AI for the next billion" actually looks like — pedagogy plus code-switching, in classrooms, not on Twitter.

cards.h4: TNEB's solar-grid forecasting AI is now load-balancing 12 GW in real time
cards.body: The Tamil Nadu electricity board has put a homegrown forecasting model into production across its solar belt. Built with IIT-Madras, it's cutting curtailment by an estimated 9% — translating to roughly ₹400 crore a year in avoided losses, and one of the largest energy-AI deployments in Asia.
cards.why_you: India's energy transition is going to be won or lost on forecasting, not panels. This is the boring infrastructure layer where the real work is.

cards.h4: Hyderabad-based Sagar Defence Engineering raises ₹220 cr for autonomous maritime AI
cards.body: Quiet Series B. The company is building autonomous surface and underwater vessels with on-board vision models tuned for Indian Ocean conditions — including the cluttered, low-visibility coastal waters where Western models trained on clean-sea datasets fail.
cards.why_you: the moat here isn't the vessel — it's the Indian Ocean dataset. Western vision models trained on clean-sea footage are useless in monsoon silt; locally-collected training data is the unfair advantage, and the people who can label and curate it are about to get hired hard.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: We hand out the word frontier when a model crosses a benchmark written in someone else's language, by someone else's exam board, for someone else's user. A model isn't frontier for India until an autorickshaw driver in Indore can argue with it in his own Hindi — switch to Marathi for a number, back to Hindi for a joke — and feel heard the whole way through. Until that day, "state-of-the-art" is just American AI passing American exams in a language none of our users were typing in to begin with.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Claude 4.8's announcement is full of context-window vocabulary. Here it is, decoded.

terms.plain: How much the AI can hold in its head at once for a single conversation. Bigger window = more notes it can read before answering. Claude 4.8's is large enough for a small book.
How the AI decides which words in your message matter most. Like which sentence in a long email actually carries the request.
Low-Rank Adaptation. Cheap way to fine-tune a big model — you only update a small slice, so it costs a fraction of full retraining and ships in hours, not weeks.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning. The family of techniques (LoRA is one) for adapting a big model without burning the GPU farm.
When teaching an AI a new skill makes it lose an old one. Like editing one chapter of a textbook and accidentally rewriting the previous ones.
Mix of Hindi + English in one sentence. Most Indian internet users write this way; most frontier models still split or break on it.
A standardized test set for Indian-language AI. The benchmark community's way of asking whether a model actually works in Hindi, Tamil, Bengali, and friends.
